questionText: A watch goes fast by 15 minutes compared to the right time every day. If it is corrected and set to the standard time at 12 o'clock at noon, which of the following will be the time shown by it at 4:00 a.m. in the morning?

steps.explanation: Calculate the duration from 12 noon to 4:00 a.m.
Find how much the watch gains in 16 hours given it gains 15 minutes in 24 hours.
Add the gained time to the actual time of 4:00 a.m.

solution.shortcut: Gain per hour = 15/24 min. Multiply by hours elapsed.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that the watch is FAST so the shown time is AHEAD of actual time.

questionText: It is between 3 P.M. and 4 P.M. and the distance between the hour and the minute hand of the clock is 18 minute spaces. What time does the clock show?

steps.explanation: At 3 o'clock the minute hand is 15 minute spaces behind the hour hand.
For the hands to be 18 minute spaces apart (minute hand behind the hour hand), the minute hand must gain (15 + 18) = 33 minute spaces.
The minute hand gains 55 minute spaces over the hour hand in 60 minutes.
Therefore the clock shows 3:36 P.M.

solution.shortcut: Minute hand gains 55 min spaces in 60 min. Set up gain equation from starting position at 3 o'clock.
solution.commonMistake: Subtracting 18 from 15 instead of adding (the minute hand is behind so must gain more).

questionText: At what time between 9 and 10 o'clock are the hands of a clock 23 minute spaces apart?

steps.explanation: At 9 o'clock the minute hand is 45 minute spaces behind the hour hand.
For the hands to be 23 minute spaces apart (with minute hand still behind), it must gain (45 − 23) = 22 minute spaces.
Time taken to gain 22 minute spaces.
The hands are 23 minute spaces apart at 9:24.

solution.shortcut: Starting gap at 9 = 45 min spaces. Required separation from behind = 45 − 23 = 22 spaces to gain.
solution.commonMistake: Using 45 + 23 = 68 instead of 45 − 23 when the minute hand hasn't yet passed the required position.

questionText: How much does a watch lose per day, if its hands coincide every 64 minutes?

steps.explanation: In a correct clock the minute hand gains 55 minute spaces over the hour hand in 60 minutes. So the hands coincide every 60/55 × 60 = 720/11 minutes ≈ 65 5/11 minutes.
Loss per coincidence interval = 720/11 − 64 = 720/11 − 704/11 = 16/11 minutes.

solution.shortcut: Normal coincidence every 720/11 min. Difference per cycle × (1440/64) gives daily loss.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting the normal coincidence interval is 720/11 min (not 65 min exactly).

questionText: At what time, in minutes, between 3 o'clock and 4 o'clock will both the needles coincide each other?

steps.explanation: At 3 o'clock, the minute hand is 15 minute spaces behind the hour hand.
To coincide, the minute hand must gain 15 minute spaces. The minute hand gains 55 spaces in 60 minutes.

solution.shortcut: Coincidence time = (60/55) × gap at start of hour.
solution.commonMistake: Dividing 60 by 60 instead of 55 for the minute hand's relative gain.

questionText: At what time between 9 and 10 o'clock will the hands of a watch be together?

steps.explanation: At 9 o'clock the minute hand is 45 minute spaces behind the hour hand.
Minute hand must gain 45 spaces at a rate of 55 spaces per 60 minutes.

solution.shortcut: Coincidence time past H o'clock = (60/55) × (5H) minutes.
solution.commonMistake: Rounding 49 1/11 to 49 or 50 without retaining the fraction.

questionText: At what time between 7 and 8 o'clock will the hands of a clock be in the same straight line but not together?

steps.explanation: Hands are in the same straight line but not together when they are 30 minute spaces apart.
At 7 o'clock, the hands are 25 minute spaces apart. The minute hand has to gain only (30 − 25) = 5 more minute spaces to reach 30 spaces apart.
Time to gain 5 minute spaces.

solution.shortcut: Straight line (opposite) means 30 min spaces apart. At 7 o'clock gap is already 25, so only 5 more spaces needed.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing 'same straight line not together' (30 spaces) with coincidence (0 spaces).

questionText: At what time between 4 and 5 o'clock will the hands of a watch point in opposite directions?

steps.explanation: At 4 o'clock, the hands are 20 minute spaces apart. For opposite directions they must be 30 minute spaces apart.
steps.calculation: Gap needed = 30 min spaces; current = 20 spaces

steps.explanation: The minute hand must gain (20 + 30) = 50 minute spaces over the hour hand.
Time to gain 50 minute spaces.

solution.shortcut: Opposite direction = 30 spaces apart. Starting gap at 4 = 20 spaces. Total to gain = 20 + 30 = 50.
solution.commonMistake: Subtracting instead of adding the 30 spaces to the initial gap.

questionText: At what time between 5.30 and 6 will the hands of a clock be at right angles?

steps.explanation: At 5 o'clock, the hands are 25 minute spaces apart. For right angles they must be 15 minute spaces apart.
Since the time is between 5.30 and 6, the minute hand has already passed the hour hand. The minute hand must gain (25 + 15) = 40 minute spaces.
Time to gain 40 minute spaces.

solution.shortcut: Right angle between 5.30 and 6 requires the minute hand to gain 40 spaces from starting position at 5.
solution.commonMistake: Choosing the earlier right-angle position (before 5:30) instead of the one in the specified range.

questionText: Henry started a trip into the country between 8 A.M. and 9 A.M. when the hands of the clock were together. He arrived at his destination between 2 P.M. and 3 P.M. when the hands of the clock were exactly 180° apart. How long did he travel?

steps.explanation: Between 8 A.M. and 9 A.M., the minute hand must gain 40 minute spaces to coincide with the hour hand.
steps.result: Henry started at $43\dfrac{7}{11}$ min past 8 A.M.

steps.explanation: Between 2 P.M. and 3 P.M., for hands to be 180° apart, they must be 30 minute spaces apart. At 2 P.M. the hands are 10 minute spaces apart. Minute hand must gain (10 + 30) = 40 minute spaces.
steps.result: Henry arrived at $43\dfrac{7}{11}$ min past 2 P.M.

steps.explanation: Duration = time from 43 7/11 min past 8 A.M. to 43 7/11 min past 2 P.M. = exactly 6 hours.

solution.shortcut: Since both departure and arrival have identical fractional minutes past their respective hours, the travel time is exactly 2 PM − 8 AM = 6 hours.
solution.commonMistake: Failing to recognise that the fractional minutes cancel out when computing the duration.

questionText: Imagine that your watch was correct at noon, but then it began to lose 30 minutes each hour. It now shows 4 p.m. but it stopped 5 hours ago. What is the correct time now?

steps.explanation: The watch loses 30 minutes every hour, meaning for every 1 hour of real time, the watch advances only 30 minutes. So for every 30 minutes the watch shows, 1 real hour passes.
The watch shows 4 p.m., meaning it advanced 4 hours (240 min) from noon. Real time elapsed = 240 × 2 = 480 min = 8 hours. So real time when watch stopped = 12 noon + 8 hours = 8 p.m.
steps.calculation: 240 min watch time × 2 = 480 min = 8 hours real. Stopped at 8 p.m.
steps.result: Watch stopped at 8 p.m. real time

steps.explanation: The watch stopped 5 hours ago (real time), so current time = 8 p.m. + 5 hours = 1 a.m.

solution.shortcut: Watch at half speed: 4 p.m. on watch = 8 hours real. Stopped at 8 p.m. + 5 hours stopped = 1 a.m.
solution.commonMistake: Not doubling the watch-shown elapsed time to get real elapsed time.

questionText: A mechanical grandfather clock is at present showing 7 hr 40 min 6 sec. Assuming that it loses 4 seconds in every hour, what time will it show after exactly $6\dfrac{1}{2}$ hours?

steps.explanation: Find the total time lost in 6.5 hours at 4 seconds per hour.
Correct time after 6.5 hours = 7 hr 40 min 6 sec + 6 hr 30 min = 14 hr 10 min 6 sec.
Subtract the time lost from the correct time to get what the clock shows.

solution.shortcut: Add actual elapsed time to current display, then subtract total seconds lost.
solution.commonMistake: Adding 26 seconds instead of subtracting (the clock LOSES time, so it shows less).

questionText: I have two watches with a 12-hour cycle. One of them gains one minute a day and the other loses $1\dfrac{1}{2}$ minutes per day. If I set them both at the correct time, how long will it be before they again tell the correct time together?

steps.explanation: A watch on a 12-hour cycle shows the correct time again after gaining or losing exactly 720 minutes (12 hours).
Watch 1 (gains 1 min/day) shows correct time after 720 days.
Watch 2 (loses 3/2 min/day) shows correct time after 720 ÷ (3/2) = 480 days.
Both show correct time together at LCM(720, 480).

solution.shortcut: Each watch must gain/lose 12 hours (720 min) to show correct time again. Find LCM of individual periods.
solution.commonMistake: Using 24 hours (1440 min) instead of 12 hours (720 min) for a 12-hour cycle clock.

modelName: Two Clocks Show Correct Time Together

questionText: A watch is 1 minute slow at 1 p.m. on Tuesday and 2 minutes fast at 1 p.m. on Thursday. When did it show the correct time?

steps.explanation: Time from 1 p.m. Tuesday to 1 p.m. Thursday = 48 hours. The watch goes from −1 min to +2 min, gaining 3 minutes in 48 hours.
Rate of gain = 3/48 = 1/16 min per hour. The watch was 1 minute slow at Tuesday 1 p.m., so it needs to gain 1 minute to show correct time.
16 hours after 1 p.m. Tuesday = 5 a.m. on Wednesday.

solution.shortcut: Total gain = slow + fast = 1 + 2 = 3 min over 48 hours. Time to gain 1 min (to correct) = 48/3 = 16 hrs after Tuesday 1 pm.
solution.commonMistake: Adding only 2 min instead of 1+2=3 min for total change across the period.

formulas.title: Time to show correct
formulas.expression: t = \frac{\text{initial error}}{\text{rate of gain/loss per hour}}

questionText: A watch which gains uniformly is 2 minutes low at noon on Monday and is 4 min 48 sec fast at 2 p.m. on the following Monday. When was it correct?

steps.explanation: Time from 12 p.m. Monday to 2 p.m. the following Monday = 7 days 2 hours = 170 hours.
Total gain over 170 hours = 2 + 4 min 48 sec = 2 + 4.8 = 6.8 min = 34/5 min.
Time to gain 2 minutes (to go from −2 to correct).
50 hours after 12 p.m. Monday = 2 p.m. Wednesday.

solution.shortcut: Total gain = 2 + 4(4/5) = 34/5 min in 170 hr. Time to gain 2 min = 2/(34/5) × 170 = 50 hr after Monday noon.
solution.commonMistake: Not converting 4 min 48 sec to minutes: 48 sec = 4/5 min, so total = 4 4/5 min not 4.8 min exactly (same value but easy to mis-compute).

questionText: A watch which gains 5 seconds in 3 minutes was set right at 7 a.m. In the afternoon of the same day, when the watch indicated quarter past 4 o'clock, the true time is

steps.explanation: The watch gains 5 sec in 3 min, meaning 3 min 5 sec of this watch = 3 min of correct clock. Equivalently, 37/720 hours of this watch = 1/20 hours of correct clock.
steps.calculation: 3 min 5 sec = 185 sec; 3 min = 180 sec. Ratio = 185/180 = 37/36.

steps.explanation: Watch showed 4:15 pm, so watch elapsed time from 7 a.m. = 9 hr 15 min = 37/4 hours.
Real elapsed time = watch elapsed time × (36/37).
True time = 7 a.m. + 9 hours = 4 p.m.

solution.shortcut: 37 min 5 sec on fast watch = 3 min real; scale down elapsed watch time to get real elapsed.
solution.commonMistake: Multiplying by 37/36 instead of dividing (the watch is fast, so real time is less than watch time).

questionText: Between 5 and 6, a lady looked at her watch and mistaking the hour hand for the minute hand, she thought that the time was 57 minutes earlier than the correct time. The correct time was

steps.explanation: The lady read the hour hand as the minute hand, giving a time 57 minutes earlier. This means the hour hand position (read as minutes) was (60 − 57) = 3 minute spaces behind the minute hand.
steps.result: Hour hand is 3 min spaces behind minute hand

steps.explanation: At 5 o'clock, the minute hand is 25 minute spaces behind the hour hand. For the minute hand to be only 3 minute spaces ahead (i.e., hour hand 3 behind minute), minute hand must gain (25 − 3) = 22 minute spaces.
Time to gain 22 minute spaces.

solution.shortcut: Swap hands mentally: hour hand read as minute hand gives 57-min error, meaning real gap = 60 − 57 = 3 spaces. Solve for when this is the gap.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that the hour hand is behind the minute hand, so we subtract (not add) 3 from 25.

questionText: How much does a watch lose per day if its hands coincide every 64 minutes?

steps.explanation: In a correct clock, hands coincide every 720/11 minutes ≈ 65 5/11 minutes.
Since the watch coincides every 64 minutes (less than 720/11), the watch is running slow (losing time).
In 720/11 correct minutes, the watch only shows 64 minutes. Loss per correct interval = 720/11 − 64 = 16/11 min.
steps.calculation: 16/11 min per 64 min of real time (approx)

steps.explanation: Loss per day (24 hours = 1440 min).

solution.commonMistake: Not using the correct normal interval of 720/11 min.

formulas.title: Daily loss formula
formulas.expression: \left(\frac{720}{11} - C\right) \times \frac{1440}{C} \text{ where C = actual coincidence interval}

questionText: A wall clock gains 2 minutes in 12 hours, while a table clock loses 2 minutes every 36 hours. Both are set right at 12 noon on Tuesday. The correct time when both show the same time next would be

steps.explanation: Wall clock gains 2 min in 12 hr = 4 min per day. Table clock loses 2 min in 36 hr = (2/36)×24 = 4/3 min per day.
For both clocks to show the same time, the total difference must be 12 hours = 720 minutes (since 12-hour clocks).

After 135 days from 12 noon Tuesday, the correct time is 12 noon (same time of day, 135 days later). Verify: wall clock gains 4×135 = 540 min; table clock loses (4/3)×135 = 180 min. Total difference = 720 min = 12 hours — both show the same time.

solution.shortcut: Combined divergence rate = gain + loss per day. Divide 720 min by rate to get days.
solution.commonMistake: Using 24-hour difference (1440 min) instead of 12-hour (720 min) for clocks showing same face.

questionText: The angle between the hands of a clock when the time is 4:25 am is

steps.explanation: Use the formula: Angle = |30H − 5.5M| where H = hours and M = minutes.
The angle is $17\dfrac{1}{2}°$.

solution.commonMistake: Using 5M instead of 5.5M for the minute hand contribution.

questionText: At 8:30, the hour hand and the minute hand of a clock form an angle of

steps.explanation: At 8:30, use the formula Angle = |30H − 5.5M|.

solution.commonMistake: Computing 30×8.5 = 255° for the hour hand position at 8:30, then 180° for minute hand — both valid methods but must subtract correctly.

questionText: A clock is displaying correct time at 9 am on Monday. If the clock loses 12 minutes in 24 hours, then the actual time when the clock indicates 8:30 pm on Wednesday of the same week is

steps.explanation: Time interval on the clock from 9 am Monday to 8:30 pm Wednesday = 59 hours 30 min = 119/2 hours.
The clock loses 12 min in 24 hr, so it shows 23 hr 48 min = 1428 min = 119/5 hours for every 24 real hours.
Real time elapsed when clock shows 119/2 hours elapsed.
Actual time = 9 am Monday + 60 hours = 9 pm Wednesday.

solution.shortcut: Clock interval / real interval = (24−12)/24 = 119/120. Real time = clock time / (119/120) = clock time × 120/119.
solution.commonMistake: Dividing instead of multiplying by the correction factor, or using the wrong direction of correction.

questionText: There are two clocks, both set to show 10 pm on 21st January 2010. One clock gains 2 minutes in an hour and the other clock loses 5 minutes in an hour. Then by how many minutes do the two clocks differ at 4 pm on 22nd January 2010?

steps.explanation: Time period from 10 pm on 21st Jan to 4 pm on 22nd Jan = 18 hours.
steps.calculation: 24 − 10 + 16 = 18 hours (10 pm to 4 pm next day)

steps.explanation: Clock 1 gains 2 min/hr, so in 18 hours it is 2 × 18 = 36 minutes fast.
Clock 2 loses 5 min/hr, so in 18 hours it is 5 × 18 = 90 minutes slow.
Total difference between the two clocks = 36 + 90 = 126 minutes.

solution.shortcut: (Gain rate + Loss rate) × hours = (2 + 5) × 18 = 126 min.
solution.commonMistake: Subtracting instead of adding the two errors — both diverge from real time in opposite directions so their difference is the sum.

formulas.title: Difference between two clocks
formulas.expression: (r_1 + r_2) \times T \text{ where } r_1 \text{ = gain rate, } r_2 \text{ = loss rate}
